title: Privacy & Anonymization
concept: Privacy protection in vision AI requires anonymizing PII (Personally Identifiable Information) via blurring, redaction, and local edge processing.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is fairness auditing in machine learning?

options.text: Splitting validation metrics by demographics to check for accuracy gaps
Ensuring the model weights are encrypted on disk
Confirming the dataset contains 1 million images

exercises.explain: Auditing demographic subsets ensures performance is fair and equitable across all user categories.

cards.title: Protecting the face in the crowd
cards.body: When a street-view car drives around taking 360-degree photos, it captures thousands of private citizens, license plates, and home windows. How do we share these maps without violating privacy? We use computer vision to blur them automatically!

cards.title: Anonymization & Privacy
cards.body: Privacy in AI focus on protecting Personally Identifiable Information (PII). Computer vision systems achieve this using automated face blurring, license plate redaction, and local edge-only processing (zero data storage).

cards.title: A digital mask generator
cards.body: Think of anonymization like a digital masquerade ball. Before anyone enters the public room (database), the AI places a mask (blur) over their face and license plate, so they can participate without revealing their identity.

cards.title: Google Street View blurring
cards.body: Google uses object detectors (YOLO-style) specialized for faces and license plates. Every time a face or plate is detected in street imagery, a Gaussian blur filter is applied directly to those pixels before storage.

cards.title: Cloud data is always a privacy risk
cards.body: You might think encrypting database files is enough to ensure privacy. In reality, if raw, unblurred video feeds are transmitted to cloud servers, they are vulnerable to intercepts and hacks. Processing locally on the edge is far more secure.

cards.title: Raw vs. Anonymized Imagery
cards.body: Applying privacy safeguards to public data:

left.text:
Clear visible faces (PII)
Legible vehicle license plates
High privacy violation risk
Subject to privacy laws (GDPR)

right.text:
Gaussian blurred faces
Redacted license plate numbers
PII removed before storage
GDPR-compliant data

exercises.prompt: What is the primary goal of face blurring in public mapping datasets?

options.text: To protect individuals' privacy by removing Personally Identifiable Information (PII)
To make the image files compress to a smaller size
To train the model to look at background colors only
To speed up edge processing times

exercises.explain: Blurring removes PII like faces, preventing individuals from being identified without their consent.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Processing camera data locally on the edge without saving or transmitting raw video is a powerful privacy safeguard.
exercises.explain: True. If raw video never leaves the edge device and is immediately discarded, the risk of data leakage is minimized.

exercises.sentence: Applying a mathematical smoothing filter to hide license plates is called plate ___.
exercises.explain: Redaction (or blurring) obscures sensitive text or pixels to protect privacy.

exercises.prompt: Which privacy regulation heavily restricts how companies collect and process biometric data like faces?
exercises.explain: GDPR in Europe (and similar laws globally) enforces strict rules on the capture and use of biometric and personal data.

exercises.prompt: You are designing a traffic camera system to count cars in a city. To comply with privacy laws (like GDPR), you must ensure no personal data is stored. Which architecture is best?

options.text: Run vehicle counting locally on an edge processor, delete the raw frames immediately, and only transmit the final count integer to the server.
Upload raw high-definition video of all cars to a public cloud database.
Store video frames locally on the camera SD card in plain view without password protection.
Use facial recognition on drivers to count the cars.

exercises.explain: Edge-only processing (analyzing and deleting raw data locally) is the gold standard for privacy-by-design, ensuring no PII is transmitted or leaked.
